entry.title: Re: SBPA Destination Error: Destination not found.
entry.content:
Hi @Abderrahamane_Kermiche ,

To trigger a workflow in SAP Build Process Automation, a destination needs to be configured in SAP BTP with the name sap_process_automation_service_user_access.

I have created a blog post explaining this setup in detail—you can refer to it here:
Trigger SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from... - SAP Community

I have also attached the destination configuration file, which you can import into your SAP BTP account.
Note: Please make sure to update the following values after import:

  • Client ID
  • Client Secret
  • Token URL

(You can find these details in the service key created for your SAP Build Process Automation instance.)
Feel free to reach out if you face any issues.

Best regards,
Abirami T

entry.title: Assignment UUID for Deep Links in SAP SuccessFactors Latest People Profile
entry.content:
Assignment UUID for Deep Links in SAP SuccessFactors Latest People Profile

Introduction

With recent releases and the move to the Latest People Profile, many existing deep links in SAP SuccessFactors that navigate to a specific employee section or portlet in the legacy profile may no longer work as expected or may not work at all.

Previously, navigation to a specific employee was commonly done using the User ID (users-sys-id). With the latest People Profile, navigation is now based on Assignment UUID.

This is further detailed in the SAP documentation regarding deep links for profile categories and cards: Deep Links to Full Profile Categories and Cards 

Scenario

URL Format

Go to a specific category for a specific user

https://<app-server-domain>/sf/liveprofile?assignmentId=<assignment UUID>&categoryId=<category Id>

This example links to a user's Job Data category:

Example

https://www.example.com/sf/liveprofile?assignmentId=B2F70B823E3B4AD08091F6D1FBB74313&categoryId=jobData

If the <assignment UUID> is not specified in the URL, the deep link defaults to the signed-in user's profile rather than the intended employee's profile.

In this blog, we focus on how to navigate to a specific employee using the Assignment UUID, which is not readily available in SAP SuccessFactors and must be fetched using Integration Center for deep links.

Understanding User Identifiers in SAP SuccessFactors

SAP SuccessFactors uses multiple identifiers to represent a person and employment data in the system.

A full overview of the available Different User Identifiers in SAP SuccessFactors is available here: Different User Identifiers in SAP SuccessFactors

Assignment UUID

Field

Description

Assignment UUID

Assignment UUIDs are 32 characters and use all uppercase letters (for example: FDF7921CD3C242F59963B30E8895D04A). They are system-generated identifiers and are used in URLs and APIs without exposing personal employee data.

Can it be changed?

No

Can it be used for SSO?

No

Can it be used for integration?

Yes

Where is it displayed on People Profile?

NA

Making Assignment UUID Available for Dynamic Deep Links

The goal is to use the Assignment UUID as a dynamic token in deep links that navigate directly to an employee profile in SAP SuccessFactors.

To achieve this, the Assignment UUID must first be fetched and made available for reuse in templates or notifications. We can implement a custom solution using the Metadata Framework (MDF) and Integration Center.

Custom MDF to Store Assignment UUID

A custom MDF object is created to store the Assignment UUID. This allows the value to be reused across different use cases.

Fields include:

  • User ID
  • Assignment UUID

View of the Custom MDF object definition in Configure Object Definitions.

Integration Center Job to Populate MDF

An Integration Center job is used to fetch the Assignment UUID and populate the custom MDF object. This job should be configured to run daily to ensure that newly hired employees are included.

Integration Center field mapping, Assignment UUID mapped to the custom MDF object field.

After the Integration Center job execution, the MDF is successfully populated, as shown below. Each record now contains the corresponding Assignment UUID, ready for use in dynamic deep links.

"Manage Data" view of the custom MDF object showing records successfully populated with User IDs and their corresponding Assignment UUIDs.

Assignment UUID: 249B9F319A8B47FBA16B6537D529E7CA

Use Assignment UUID as a Dynamic Token in Deep Links

Once stored, the Assignment UUID can be used as a dynamic token in a URL in Document Generation via rule mapping, and when the notification is generated, the token is replaced dynamically.

Example deep link:

https://hcm-xxx.hr.cloud.sap/sf/liveprofile?assignmentId=[[AssignmentUUID]]&categoryId=jobData

Document Generation template displaying the alert message, with the clickable link and the [[AssignmentUUID]] token shown separately to illustrate dynamic value population.

Document Generation template editor displaying the [[AssignmentUUID]] token embedded within the URL link configuration.

Document Mapping Rule (Lookup Function)

Create a rule with a Document Mapping scenario to retrieve the Assignment UUID from MDF using a lookup function.

Business Rule using a Lookup() function to fetch the Assignment UUID from the custom MDF object..

Reference Rule in Document Template Mapping

Document Generation Mapping screen where the [[AssignmentUUID]] token is assigned to the Business Rule

Notifications with Assignment UUID Deep Links

The notification now generates a dynamic link with the Assignment UUID and directs the user to the corresponding employee profile in the Latest People Profile.

The email notification: 

Email notification displaying the clickable deep link populated with the dynamic Assignment UUID.

The Link:
https://hcm-xxx.hr.cloud.sap/sf/liveprofile?assignmentId=249B9F319A8B47FBA16B6537D529E7CA&categoryId=jobData

Successful Navigation to Employee Profile

Employee profile Job Data section displayed after clicking the deep link, confirming successful navigation using the dynamic Assignment UUID.

Summary 

This blog explained how to fetch the Assignment UUID and use it in URL links to navigate to a specific employee profile, category, or card in the Latest People Profile in SAP SuccessFactors. Since this identifier is not directly available in the system, a custom solution is implemented to retrieve and maintain it for dynamic use.

The solution is implemented as follows:

  • The Assignment UUID is retrieved using an Integration Center job and stored in a custom MDF object
  • A business rule with a lookup function dynamically fetches the value
  • The Assignment UUID is then used as a token and embedded into deep link URLs in the document generation template

Notes & Key Considerations

  • Job Frequency: Ensure your Integration Center job runs frequently enough to account for new hires.
  • URL Encoding: When building URLs, ensure there are no trailing spaces or characters that could break the string.

entry.title: Re: RBP Permissions have been affected with the new peopl...
entry.content: Okay, I have gone through the links mentioned and it clearly says "If you are an existing customer, you don't need to set up category access controls from the beginning. Starting with 1H 2026 release, the system automatically migrates your category-level access configurations and generates permission items for those categories with target roles assigned, including their role assignments in the People Profile Categories permission list. Permissions are not generated for categories that were visible to everyone. No changes are made to any existing card or field permissions, and these permission configurations remain the same after migration." As per this, it should have automatically set up those permissions according to the old people profile.

entry.title: Re: Errors while new hiring
entry.content:
The error usually occurs due to data mismatch or invalid foundation object values, not just configuration.

Even if associations are correctly maintained, the system validates whether the Company, Division, and Department are active, valid as of the hire date, and correctly aligned in the hierarchy (Department → Division → Company). If any of these do not align—for example, if the Department is not linked to the selected Division or the effective dates do not match—the system throws this error.

Please verify the foundation object records, their hierarchy relationships, and effective dates in Manage Data.

entry.title: Re: The function module CONVERT_BDCMSGCOLL_TO_BAPIRET2 do...
entry.content:
As per note 3411865 - Dump: CONVERT_BDCMSGCOLL_TO_BAPIRET2 not found in the transaction /DBE/CASHDESK a call to the CONVERT_BDCMSGCOLL_TO_BAPIRET2 function can be replaced by the following code:

  FIELD-SYMBOLS:
    <ls_bdcmsgcoll> LIKE LINE OF pt_messtab[].              

*  CALL FUNCTION 'CONVERT_BDCMSGCOLL_TO_BAPIRET2'
*    TABLES
*      imt_bdcmsgcoll = pt_messtab
*      ext_return     = pt_return.
  LOOP AT pt_messtab[] ASSIGNING <ls_bdcmsgcoll>.
    sy-msgty = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgtyp.
    sy-msgid = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgid.
    sy-msgno = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgnr.
    sy-msgv1 = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgv1(50).
    sy-msgv2 = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgv2(50).
    sy-msgv3 = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgv3(50).
    sy-msgv4 = <ls_bdcmsgcoll>-msgv4(50).
    CALL FUNCTION 'BALW_BAPIRETURN_GET2'
         EXPORTING
              type             = sy-msgty
              cl               = sy-msgid
              number           = sy-msgno
              par1             = sy-msgv1
              par2             = sy-msgv2
              par3             = sy-msgv3
              par4             = sy-msgv4
         IMPORTING
              return           = ls_return.
    APPEND ls_return TO pt_return[].
  ENDLOOP.
  UNASSIGN <ls_bdcmsgcoll>.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Build Work Zone: No Content Items Created from S/4HANA FLP Content Exposure (0/0)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently integrating SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition) with an SAP S/4HANA on‑premise system using Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP (Exposure Version 2).

Although the content exposure in S/4HANA seems to be set up correctly, no content items are created in Build Work Zone after channel synchronization.

Observed Behavior

  • The FLP Content Exposure runs successfully in S/4HANA
  • JSON content is generated for:
    • Business Apps
    • Catalogs
    • Groups
    • Roles
  • In Build Work Zone → Channel Manager, the channel status shows Updated
  • However, the Channel Report shows:

    Content Items: 0 / 0
    No content items were created or updated

Expected Behavior

  • Catalogs, apps, and groups exposed from S/4HANA should be converted into content items in Build Work Zone and become available for site usage.

Configuration Details

  • S/4HANA FLP content includes:
    • Business apps (homepage, search)
    • Catalogs assigned to roles
    • Role created specifically for Build Work Zone consumption
  • Content exposure uses Exposure Version 2
  • Channel is created in Build Work Zone in automatic mode
  • Cloud connector and destination setup is in place

Checks Already Done

  • Verified that FLP catalogs are assigned to the role
  • Verified that the role is included in content exposure
  • Channel synchronization completes without errors
  • No errors in BWZ logs, only empty content result

Questions

  1. Which technical roles are mandatory for the S/4HANA user (or technical integration user) to allow Build Work Zone to read FLP content?
  2. Is any additional role required for content item generation?
  3. Are there additional authorization objects (e.g. /UI2/FLP_CONT) that are required?
  4. Are there known prerequisites or limitations when using Exposure Version 2 with Build Work Zone?
  5. Any checks or logs you would recommend to identify why content items are not being generated?

Any guidance or pointers would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Please find the screenshots below for your reference.

Kind regards,
Om Prakash Jha

entry.title: SAP S4HANA ist live – jetzt beginnt der echte Change - Recap der April Kaffee-Ecke
entry.content:
Die SAP Training & Change Management Community traf sich zur „Kaffeeecke" – dem regelmäßigen Austauschformat, moderiert von Seyde Sosnovski und mir. Zu Gast war Torben Draeger, Stream Lead Record to Report bei Sysmex Europe in Hamburg. Er berichtete sehr offen über die Herausforderungen nach dem Go-Live von S/4HANA – und darüber, warum der echte Change erst nach dem Projekt beginnt.

Sysmex: Ein Hidden Champion der Medizintechnik

Sysmex Corporation wurde 1968 in Kobe, Japan, gegründet. Das Unternehmen produziert medizinische Analysegeräte – unter anderem für die Hämatologie, Hämostase und Urindiagnostik. Die Geräte unterstützen Labore und Krankenhäuser weltweit bei der Krebsdiagnostik. Sysmex ist in fünf Regionen organisiert und betreibt über 80 Tochtergesellschaften mit rund 12.000 Mitarbeitenden. Das europäische Headquarter sitzt in Hamburg.

Greenfield statt Brownfield: Eine bewusste Entscheidung

Als das japanische Headquarter die S/4HANA-Einführung anstieß, entschied sich Sysmex gemeinsam mit dem Implementierungspartner EY für einen Greenfield-Ansatz. Das Ziel: Harmonisierung und Standardisierung. Statt Altlasten aus dem bisherigen ECC-System mitzunehmen, sollte ein komplett neues System auf Basis des SAP-Standards aufgebaut werden. Die ursprüngliche Planung sah anderthalb Jahre vor. Tatsächlich dauerte das Projekt rund fünf Jahre, bis alle Tochtergesellschaften weltweit live waren. 

Torben Draeger machte kein Geheimnis daraus, dass der Greenfield-Ansatz die Mitarbeitenden vor erhebliche Herausforderungen stellte. Individuell gewachsene Prozesse wurden durch standardisierte Abläufe ersetzt. Gleichzeitig wurde SAP Fiori als alleinige Benutzeroberfläche eingeführt – der klassische SAP GUI wurde komplett abgelöst. Im Rückblick würde Draeger diesen Schritt wieder so machen, empfiehlt aber, den Übergang sanfter zu gestalten: Erst das Fiori Launchpad einführen, die gewohnten Transaktionen beibehalten und dann schrittweise ausgewählte (einfache, gut funktionierende 🙂 Fiori-Apps ergänzen.

Nach dem Projekt ist vor dem Projekt

Seit rund einem Jahr ist Sysmex mit allen Tochtergesellschaften produktiv auf S/4HANA. Doch der Go-Live war nicht das Ende, sondern der Beginn einer neuen Phase. Torben beschrieb den aktuellen Zustand mit einem Bild: Man hat ein Haus gebaut – mit Smarthome-Technik, PV-Anlage und automatischer Garage. Jetzt geht es darum, dieses Haus zu pflegen und weiterzuentwickeln, ohne es zu "verhunzen".

Konkret steht Sysmex vor einem Spagat: Einerseits soll der erarbeitete Standard bewahrt werden. Andererseits müssen Verbesserungen möglich bleiben. Draeger formuliert drei zentrale Aufgaben:

  • Bewahren: Die Ergebnisse aus fünf Jahren harter Arbeit schützen. Keine unkontrollierten Änderungen am System zulassen.
  • Verbessern: Neue SAP-Funktionen prüfen und einführen, wo sie Mehrwert bringen.
  • Verstehen: Aktiv zuhören, ob Prozesse funktionieren. Regelmäßig mit den Fachabteilungen im Austausch bleiben.

Die Dreifaltigkeit: Stream Leads, Senior Management und IT

Sysmex hat die Projektorganisation in den Regelbetrieb überführt. Das Unternehmen arbeitet in sogenannten Streams – thematisch organisierten Bereichen wie Order to Cash, Purchase to Pay oder Record to Report. Jeder Stream hat einen regionalen und globalen Lead.

Drei Instanzen bilden das Rückgrat der Post-Go-Live-Organisation:

  1. Stream Leads wie Torben Draeger hinterfragen jede Änderungsanforderung: Brauchen wir das wirklich? Geht das im Standard? Muss es eine Drittanbieterlösung sein?
  2. Das Senior Management ist heute eng eingebunden und trifft strategische Entscheidungen zu Investitionen und Systemarchitektur – eine Veränderung gegenüber der Zeit vor dem Projekt.
  3. Die IT hat eine umfassende Software-Validierung (CSV) eingeführt. Als Medizintechnikunternehmen unterliegt Sysmex strengen regulatorischen Vorgaben. Änderungen müssen dokumentiert und validiert werden.

Diese drei Ebenen sorgen dafür, dass Änderungen nicht mehr informell per Telefonanruf angestoßen werden, sondern einen strukturierten Prozess durchlaufen. Das schützt den Standard – überfordert aber manche Mitarbeitenden, die gewohnt waren, Anpassungen schnell und unbürokratisch zu bekommen.

Key User als Rückgrat der Organisation

Das Herzstück der Post-Go-Live-Strategie ist die Key-User-Community. Key User gibt es auf lokaler, regionaler und globaler Ebene. Sie sind das Bindeglied zwischen System, Organisation und Anwendenden. Ihre Aufgaben gehen weit über technisches Know-how hinaus:

  • Sie schulen neue Mitarbeitende – auch bei der Gründung neuer Tochtergesellschaften (zuletzt in Griechenland und Kenia).
  • Sie erklären, warum Prozesse standardisiert sind und Änderungen nicht ohne weiteres möglich sind.
  • Sie tragen Entscheidungen in die Organisation und hören gleichzeitig aktiv zu.
  • Sie dokumentieren Prozesse und Änderungen.

Die Key User im Headquarter arbeiten in Vollzeit in dieser Rolle. In den Tochtergesellschaften übernehmen Mitarbeitende die Aufgabe zusätzlich zu ihrem regulären Job – als Kreditorenbuchhalter, Einkäufer oder Produktionsmitarbeiter. Ein formaler Katalog beschreibt die Erwartungshaltung an die Rolle. Die Führungsebene benennt geeignete Kandidaten, wobei alles auf freiwilliger Basis läuft.

Bemerkenswert: Sysmex hat eher das Problem, Bewerbungen ablehnen zu müssen, als zu wenige Kandidaten zu finden. In der Community-Diskussion zeigte sich, dass dies bei vielen anderen Unternehmen anders ist – dort scheuen Mitarbeitende den zusätzlichen Aufwand.

Dokumentation und Tools

Sysmex nutzt SAP Enable Now für Schulungskurse, die von den Headquarter-Key-Usern erstellt und durchgeführt werden. Den SAP Companion für In-App-Hilfe hat das Unternehmen technisch implementiert, aber nie produktiv genutzt. Im Projekt lag der Fokus auf klassischen Schulungskursen, weil nur geschulte Mitarbeitende überhaupt testen durften. Angesichts der Ankündigung, dass SAP in Richtung WalkMe geht, hat Sysmex den Companion-Rollout vorerst gestoppt.

Die technische Dokumentation beschrieb Draeger als extrem umfangreich – typisch für die Medizintechnikbranche. Der SAP Solution Manager dient als zentrale Plattform. Rund 30–40 Prozent der IT-Arbeitszeit fließen in Dokumentation.

Kommunikation auf mehreren Ebenen

Die Kommunikation rund um SAP läuft bei Sysmex nicht nur über Key User. Es gibt eine eigenständige Kommunikationsstruktur mit Projektleitern, einer IT-Abteilung für Change Management und verschiedenen Kanälen – von Teams über E-Mail bis zum direkten Austausch. Bei strategisch wichtigen Themen wird pyramidenförmig kommuniziert: erst die Management Directors, dann die Abteilungsleiter, dann die Organisation.

Fünf spannende Aussagen aus der Session

  1. „Nach dem Projekt ist vor dem Projekt. Die Strukturen, die man im Projekt aufbaut, zu bewahren und nicht zu verlieren – das ist die größte Herausforderung." 

  2. „Wir wollen nicht wieder, dass nach einigen Jahren das System so verbastelt ist, wie es im ECC halt war." 

  3. „Die Ideen und die Vorstellung, die man mit dieser Software verbindet, die zum Leben zu erwecken und am Leben zu erhalten – das ist die größte Herausforderung für mich persönlich." 

  4. „Key User vertreten das Unternehmen, vertreten bestimmte Werte und Strategien. Das muss ja jemand rüberbringen." 

  5. „Die Leute, die im Projekt gearbeitet haben, waren nie freigestellt. Dadurch hatten wir nicht diesen harten Bruch zwischen Projekt und Regelbetrieb." 

Tipps für die Praxis
  • Key User institutionalisieren: Definiert klare Rollenbeschreibungen und Erwartungshaltungen. Lasst die Führungsebene geeignete Kandidaten benennen – auf freiwilliger Basis.
  • Standard bewahren: Richtet eine Instanz ein, die bei jeder Änderungsanforderung hinterfragt, ob der SAP-Standard nicht ausreicht.
  • Senior Management einbinden: Strategische Entscheidungen zum System gehören auf die Führungsebene – nicht nur in die IT.
  • Keinen harten Bruch zwischen Projekt und Betrieb: Wenn Key User und Projektmitarbeitende ihre reguläre Rolle behalten, gelingt der Übergang leichter.
  • Fiori gleich miteinführen: Wer Greenfield macht, sollte die Benutzeroberfläche gleich umstellen. Aber sanft: gewohnte Transaktionen beibehalten und schrittweise Fiori-Apps einführen.
  • Regelmäßig zuhören: Wöchentliche Stand-ups, Foren und Workshops mit den Fachabteilungen helfen, Probleme frühzeitig zu erkennen.
  • Dokumentation ernst nehmen: Gerade in regulierten Branchen ist lückenlose Dokumentation Pflicht – plant dafür ausreichend Kapazität ein.
 
Resümee

Die Session mit Torben zeigte eindrücklich: Der Go-Live von S/4HANA ist nicht die Ziellinie, sondern ein Startschuss. Der Change beginnt danach, bzw. ist danach immer noch ein Thema – im täglichen Spagat zwischen Standardbewahrung und Weiterentwicklung. Sysmex hat diesen Übergang geschafft, indem das Unternehmen die Projektstrukturen in den Regelbetrieb überführt, Key User als zentrale Anlaufstellen verankert und das Senior Management aktiv eingebunden hat. Perfekt läuft nicht alles, das betonte Torben. Aber die Richtung stimmt. Die wichtigste Erkenntnis: Es sind nicht die technischen Systeme, die den Unterschied machen, sondern die Menschen, die sie tragen und erklären.

📅 Nächste Kaffeeecke am 28.05. zum Thema „Business Transformation Framework": Jetzt anmelden
📅 SAP Learning & Adoption Forum in Walldorf oder online am 11.06.: Zur Registrierung
 
💬 Ihr wollt mitdiskutieren oder eine eigene Case Study vorstellen? Meldet euch bei Seyde oder mir. 

Fragen und Antworten

Die Diskussion die über 20 Minuten ging habe ich anbei nochmals als Fragen und Antworten hier dokumentiert. 

Wie ist Sysmex nach dem Go-Live organisiert?

Sysmex arbeitet in Streams – thematisch gegliederten Bereichen wie Order to Cash, Purchase to Pay oder Record to Report. Jeder Stream hat einen Lead, der auf regionaler und globaler Ebene Änderungsanforderungen prüft und den SAP-Standard schützt. Drei Instanzen arbeiten zusammen: Stream Leads, Senior Management und IT. Das Senior Management ist seit dem Projekt deutlich enger eingebunden als vorher.

Wie strukturiert ihr Feedback-Termine mit den Fachabteilungen?

Es gibt wöchentliche Stand-up-Meetings pro Stream, in denen sich Key User austauschen. Feste Protokolle gibt es nicht – das Format richtet sich situationsbedingt nach dem aktuellen Thema. Die Key User sind erfahren genug, um ihr Publikum einzuschätzen und die Kommunikation entsprechend anzupassen. Ergänzend finden Foren, Workshops und gezielte Veranstaltungen statt, um aktiv zuzuhören.

Nach welchen Kriterien wurden die Key User ausgewählt?

Sysmex hat einen Katalog erstellt, der Erwartungshaltung und Aufgaben der Key-User-Rolle beschreibt. Dieser ging an die Führungsebene der Tochtergesellschaften, die dann geeignete Kandidaten benannt haben. Alles läuft auf freiwilliger Basis. Das Unternehmen hat eher das Problem, Bewerbungen ablehnen zu müssen, als zu wenige Interessenten zu finden.

Wie viel Zeit wird Key Usern für die Rolle eingeräumt?

Im Headquarter ist die Key-User-Rolle ein Vollzeitjob. In den Tochtergesellschaften übernehmen Mitarbeitende die Aufgabe neben ihrem regulären Job. Bei konkreten Projekten – etwa einem Release-Upgrade mit umfangreichen Tests – wird der Aufwand vorab kommuniziert, zum Beispiel drei Wochen intensives Testen. Im Alltag hängt der Aufwand von der Größe der Tochtergesellschaft ab: In kleinen Einheiten ist der Key User weniger gefragt, in größeren ist er täglicher Ansprechpartner für alle SAP-Fragen.

Wie habt ihr den Übergang vom Projektmodus in den Produktmodus geschafft?

Dieser Übergang dauert bis heute an. Ein entscheidender Vorteil: Die Projektmitarbeitenden waren nie freigestellt, sondern haben ihren regulären Job parallel weitergeführt. Dadurch gab es keinen harten Bruch. Organisatorisch wurde zwischen zwei Ebenen getrennt: ein Team für technisches Change Management (Änderungen am System) und ein Team für den First-Level-Support (AMS). Änderungen aus dem Support werden im Stream bewertet und dann umgesetzt oder abgelehnt.

Wie dokumentiert ihr Änderungen am System?

Extrem umfangreich. Sysmex nutzt den SAP Solution Manager als zentrale Plattform für technische Dokumentation und Transportmanagement. Für jeden technischen Change gibt es mehrere Dokumente, die ausgefüllt, freigegeben und hochgeladen werden müssen. Rund 30–40 Prozent der IT-Arbeitszeit fließen in Dokumentation. Key User sind daran beteiligt. Für Schulungsdokumentation wird SAP Enable Now eingesetzt.

Wie ist die Key-User-Community aufgebaut und wie wird sie gemanagt?

Die Stream Leads sind in der Regel auch Teamleiter der Key-User-Gruppen im Headquarter. Torben Draeger leitet zum Beispiel das Team Finance & Controlling mit fünf bis sechs Personen und ist gleichzeitig Stream Lead für Record to Report in der Region EMEA. In den Tochtergesellschaften sind Key User keine eigene Stellenbezeichnung, sondern eine zusätzliche Rolle – etwa für Kreditorenbuchhalter oder Einkäufer. Die Abstimmung mit dem globalen Headquarter in Japan erfolgt regelmäßig.

Läuft die Kommunikation rund um SAP nur über Key User?

Nein. Es gibt eine eigenständige Kommunikationsstruktur: Projektleiter kommunizieren unabhängig, die IT hat eine eigene Change-Management-Abteilung, und es werden verschiedene Kanäle bespielt – Teams, E-Mail, direkter Austausch. Bei strategisch wichtigen Themen wird pyramidenförmig kommuniziert: zuerst Management Directors, dann Abteilungsleiter, dann die gesamte Organisation. Auch das Senior Management kommuniziert aktiv zu SAP-Strategien und Budgetentscheidungen.

Nutzt ihr den SAP Companion für In-App-Hilfe?

Technisch ist der Companion vorhanden, produktiv wird er aber nicht genutzt. Im Projekt lag der Fokus darauf, Mitarbeitende über klassische Schulungskurse in Enable Now zu qualifizieren – eine Voraussetzung, um überhaupt testen zu dürfen (CSV-Vorgabe). Nach dem Projekt war der Companion-Rollout geplant, wurde aber zurückgestellt, als SAP die Partnerschaft mit WalkMe ankündigte.

Habt ihr SAP Fiori direkt mit S/4HANA eingeführt oder erst danach?

Direkt mit dem Go-Live. Sysmex hat den SAP GUI komplett abgelöst und ist auf Fiori Only gegangen. Das war eine der größten Herausforderungen für die Anwendenden, weil gewohnte Transaktionscodes und die vertraute Oberfläche wegfielen. Im Rückblick war es die richtige Entscheidung, weil ein Folgeprojekt zur GUI-Ablösung entfiel. Draeger empfiehlt aber: Erst das Fiori Launchpad einführen, gewohnte Transaktionen beibehalten und dann schrittweise ausgewählte Fiori-Apps ergänzen.

Was ist die größte Erkenntnis nach dem Go-Live?

Die Strukturen aus dem Projekt zu bewahren ist die größte Herausforderung. Key User, Wissen und Prozesse dürfen nicht verloren gehen. Die Mitarbeitenden, die das Projekt getragen haben, sollten auch danach in ihrer Rolle bleiben. Der echte Change ist kein einmaliges Ereignis, sondern tägliche Arbeit – im Spagat zwischen Standardbewahrung und Weiterentwicklung.

Kommt gerne in die nächste Kaffee-Ecke und in das jährliche Vor-Ort Treffen - dem SAP Learning & Change Forum!

entry.title: In Brief - Product Compliance Dangerous Goods Management S/4HANA - End to End Overview
entry.content:
In Brief - Product Compliance Dangerous Goods Management - End to End Overview

Transporting dangerous goods is subject to strict regulatory requirements and increasing scrutiny across global supply chains. Non compliance can lead to serious safety risks, legal consequences, and operational disruptions.

At the same time, many organizations still rely on fragmented processes, manual checks, and inconsistent data, making it difficult to ensure compliance at scale and across functions.

In this video, we illustrate how SAP S/4HANA supports an integrated approach to Dangerous Goods Management as part of Product Compliance. By embedding compliance directly into product master data and connecting it with logistics processes, companies can ensure that relevant regulations are considered consistently from the very beginning.

This enables automated checks during order and delivery processing, improves data transparency, and helps ensure that all required information, documentation, and handling instructions are available where they are needed.

An integrated approach not only reduces compliance risks but also increases efficiency and supports reliable and safe transport execution across markets.

Watch the demo video to see how this can be realized in practice.

In Brief - Product Compliance Dangerous Goods Management - End to End Overview

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Process automation: Capturing SAP GUI 8.00 Screen fails
entry.content:
Hi @JoachimSiebert 
I came across similar cases where screen capturing does not work properly with SAP GUI 8.00.

You can try the following steps, which may help resolve the issue:

  1. Restart the Desktop Agent and try recording again.
  2. If the issue persists, open Task Manager and completely stop all running instances of the Desktop Agent (including background services). Then restart the Desktop Agent and try capturing the screen again.

  3. Check if manual screen capture works. If manual capture is also failing, the issue might be related to compatibility between the Desktop Agent and SAP GUI version.
  4. If none of the above steps work, consider using a different or updated version of the Desktop Agent.

Since you are using trail Desktop Agent version(3.32) might also be a limiting factor.

Hope this helps.

Best Regards, Abirami T

entry.title: Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store
entry.content:
What Do We Need To Size

The  SAP Datasphere Object Store consists of three features:

  1. The object store to hold data, that includes the data in the tables, metadata  logs, and back-up snapshots.
  2. The Apache Spark engine as the compute for all operations, like creating delta tables, transformations, and housekeeping.
  3. SAP HANA Native SQL on Files the SQL on files engine in SAP HANA, as an bridge between the object store and the relational world of SQL, that is used when reporting on data in the object store, when previewing it, when querying it in any fashion and more.

The SAP Datasphere Object Store meters three distinct, but dependent measures.

  • Object Store Storage - is the sum of all files and folders stored in the object store of an SAP Datasphere space.
  • Object Store Compute - is the total amount of 4 GB Memory hours (which combined CPU, Memory and temporary disk storage) used by all Spark jobs.
  • Object Store Requests - occur during all interactions with the object store, e.g. reading a file (get OPEN), writing a file (put CREATE), deleting a file (del DELETE) and many more (see documentation).

Data Volumes and Compression

We must be aware that the same records stored in a raw format, local tables (HANA) SAP HANA, or local tables (file) (stored as delta tables in SAP HANA Data Lake Files) use different amounts of storage. Compression rates can vary based on the actual data and its data types. One assumption that can be used is:

1 GB CSV (Raw) -> ~1/8 GB in HANA
1 GB HANA  -> 1.35 GB in delta tables

For the performance calculation, we will use the SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF) data volume.

Object Store Storage

The total data footprint consists of the initially loaded data plus the incremental delta loads accumulated over time.

When data is loaded into a local table (file) using a Replication Flow, it is first written to an inbound buffer. Then a merge task has to be completed so that the data is moved into the underlying delta table.

Data files in delta tables are immutable. Any update, delete, or merge operation generates new files and record ‘remove file’ actions for the old ones. As a result, overwriting data increases storage usage because previous file versions must be retained. These older files are needed for delta extraction and must remain available until all delta subscribers have consumed their data. They must be removed later by a vacuum job. The amount of storages used by these old versions depends on whether the source provides change data (updates/deletes) or only new inserts

The object store storage can be calculated by combining the initially loaded data and adding the periodic load volume that is added per load, times the frequency of the periodic load and the version retention period. In the worst-case scenario, all files receive an update during a delta load, which would cause the table to be re-written with a slightly larger size. 

Here, SAP is working on multiple improvements to help minimize this growth, see the Roadmap Item 

The best-case scenario is when only inserts are delivered and files are never changed, in which case the table storage only grows by its added volume of data during a merge.

The object store keeps a backup of all deleted files for disaster recovery over a period of 14 days.

The versions and backup should level out eventually, when the retention periods are reached and a new version comes in, as an old version leaves the system.

Storage is metered hourly. That results in the fact that the monthly CU charged is not the size at the end of the month time hours in the month, it is rather the integral over time. For a linear graph, it’s the the average data size multiplied by the number of hours in the month (for estimations we assume 730h per month). Or in other words we need to estimate the average active data size in our system.

Object Store Compute

Object Store Compute is measured in blocks, based on 4 GB/h. This is because all Apache Spark configurations currently have a 1 CPU to 4 GB memory ratio.

Therefore, we need to estimate how many hours Apache Spark will run. This is the sum of hours spent on:

  • Task runs in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • Deployments in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • System Tasks
    • Statistic Updates 
    • Housekeeping jobs (like cleanup of logs)

For merge and transformation flow tasks, the required object store compute depends on many factors:

Data Characteristics

  • Volume - total data size drives memory and parallelism needs
  • Cardinality - high-cardinality columns (e.g. user IDs) affect shuffle size in groupBy/join
  • Skew - uneven key distribution causes some tasks to process far more data than others
  • Schema width - wide rows (many columns) increase serialization overhead
  • Write pattern - insert-only workloads produce fewer redundant files than upsert/delete-heavy ones, so Optimize is cheaper after them.

Transformation Type

  • Narrow vs. wide transformations - filter, map, select stay within a partition; groupBy, join, distinct, repartition cause shuffles (expensive)
  • Shuffle operations - triggered by wide transformations; move data across the network and write intermediate results to disk
  • User defined functions (UDF) - python UDFs especially are slow (JVM ↔ Python serialization); prefer built-in Spark SQL functions

Workload Configuration

  • Executor memory/cores - determine parallelism and whether spill to disk occurs
  • Dynamic allocation - lets Spark scale executors based on workload

For optimize, the required object store compute depends on:

  • Number of small files - the main cost driver. Each write operation (merge, load, upsert) appends new files and creates new versions. The more fragmented the table, the more spark workload optimize must use to compact them. This makes optimize costs grow with write frequency, not just data size.
  • Table size (total data volume) - larger tables take longer to compact even at the same file count.
  • Z-ORDER BY - if column co-location (Z-ordering) is applied, Optimize must physically reshuffle data across files to group related rows. This is significantly more compute-intensive than simple compaction.

For an overview on best practices on optimizing consumption of the object store see the SAP Note 3722983

Object Store Requests

Object store requests are created during any read and write interaction with the object store. Besides the Spark-based tasks we need to add the object store requests from the Replication Flow writing into the inbound buffer table and we need to consider data access by SQL on File (SoF) calls.

Object store requests will be the combination of:

  • Task runs in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • Replication Flow writes into Inbound Buffer
  • Deployments in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • SQL on Files (e.g. cross-space sharing to HANA spaces or Data Preview)

Factors that impact object store requests:

  • Access method to local tables (file) (delta/active) - delta access requires additional access to logs and versions, while the volume in delta is typically smaller

Merge

  • Data volume in Inbound Buffer - determines how many target files are candidates for rewrite
  • Insert-only vs update/delete - insert-only merge can append new files; updates/deletes require rewriting matched files. When only inserts occur, it skips expensive target file rewrite

SQL on File reads into HANA

  • Total number of files in local table (file) -  fewer large files vs. many small files (influenced by frequency of merge or optimize runs)
  • Partition pruning - filters on partition columns eliminate entire directories → fewer calls
  • Column pruning - only requested columns are fetched (fewer bytes, same file count)

HANA can be used to cache data for reads, especially for SAP Analytics Cloud. Related best practice is to create data marts in spaces with SAP HANA Cloud storage (local tables (HANA) or persisted views) for reporting scenarios with frequent use and SLAs requiring high-performance.  There are ways to mitigate the full reads in SAP Datasphere persisted views . You can partition views and lock partitions that don’t change, preventing unnecessary data read. To enable this, ensure that the partitions defined in the view are aligned with the partitions defined in the Local Table (File),  so that partition locking effectively avoids reading the underlying files.

Another alternative is to use local tables (HANA) instead of view persistency.  This allows the system to load only the deltas from the Local Tables (File), so it reads only the changes. As a result, the number of object store requests is significantly reduced.

SQL on Files object store requests run by queries (such as MDS queries, previews, etc.) are even more difficult to estimate, as there is typically no available data to base these calculations on. 

For an overview on best practices on optimizing consumption of the object store see the SAP Note 3722983

Input for sizing

  • Find your average active data size
  • Estimate how many small (<5GB), medium (5GB-50GB) and large (> 50GB) tables you have and what percentage of tables have changes, and which are insert-only
  • Define data availability and update frequency for tables based on end user requirements and object store consumption. More frequent merge runs increase object store consumption.
  • Determine the number of potential re-initializations per year, for example required for incompatible schema changes in connected sources
  • Make an assumption about data retention (housekeeping on data performed with delete and subsequent vacuum processes).

If you need support to come up with an estimate, reach out to your SAP contact (Sales, Pre-Sales, COE)

entry.title: SF EC/ECP Data transformation with Integration with SAP S4/Hana Public cloud
entry.content:
BACKGROUND

We are currently migrating from on-prem ECC system to SAP SuccessFactors EC/ECP and SAP S4 /HANA Public cloud, we are implementing a coupled go-live of SuccessFactors and S/4HANA. AS per SAP’s limitation in S4/Hana Public cloud we have to use the certain default configurations to define our enterprise structure in public cloud. Because of this we can’t define our ECC controlling area in S4 and have to use the controlling are in S4 as A000.

The answer to the key question we are looking get here is whether data transformation within EC and ECP is required, and what SAP's supported approach is for this scenario.

Data specifics - The data which will be held in Employee Central and Employee Central Payroll — references the original controlling area ( from ECC – to ECP for majority of the data that includes – Past payroll results, cost distributions- table ASSHR and ASSOB,  But the data replication from the EC to ECP – updates only infotype 1 with new controlling area). We understand that when payroll data is posted from ECP to S4, the system performs an automatic conversion(We have tested that- we checked that if you modify any historical records lets say cost distribution then system changes its controlling area of teh record to new one). However, this automatic conversion does not address the historical data held within EC and ECP where we have concerns about:

  1. Retro calculations across two controlling areas: Will SAP support retro payroll calculations within ECP that span two different controlling areas (ECC old  and the new A000) without first transforming the historical records held in EC and ECP?
  2. Data integrity within EC and ECP: If historical data within Employee Central and Employee Central Payroll retains references to the old controlling area post-migration, what are the implications for data integrity within the SuccessFactors environment? Specifically, does SAP consider this a supported and clean configuration for a new system going live?
  1. Payroll posting file reconciliation: During our Parallel Payroll Run we will extract, transform, and load data from ECC into EC and ECP, then run payroll in ECP and post the results to S4. If no data transformation is applied within EC/ECP prior to this process, what risk does it have in terms of accuracy and completeness of the payroll posting file posted to S4?
  1. Audit trail and compliance: If we update historical records in EC and ECP to reflect a new controlling area that did not exist when those records were created, what guidance does SAP provide for maintaining an appropriate audit trail?
  2. SAP's recommended approach: What is SAP's recommended approach for ensuring that EC and ECP data are aligned with the new S4 controlling area structure at go-live? Is the transformation of historical data within EC/ECP a supported, recommended activity, and if so, what tooling or methodology does SAP recommend?

entry.title: Req Object is not getting passed to bound action
entry.content:
Hi all,

I wanted to build several functions for my Service in CAP. Weirdly I can access the req object for bound actions of one entity without problems, but bound actions on another entity have only association/composition arguments.

ServiceDefinition cds

entity A as projection on my.A actions {
 action doSomethingA(); //works with req!
}

entity  B as projection on my.B actions{
 action doSomethingB();//does not work with req!
}

 in javascript I then extend the ApplicationService

class TestService extends cds.ApplicationService {
 init(){
  this.on('doSomethingA','A',this.doSomethingA);
  this.on('doSomethingB','B',this.doSomethingB);
 }

 async doSomethingA(req){
  //here i have access to the req object and can use something like req.params or req.subject
 }

 async doSomethingB(entityDefinition,a,c,d,b){
  //here i do NOT have access to the req object and CAN'T use something like req.params or req.subject
  // I need to use different variables for each entity in the Association/Composition chain. so for entities related like this A->C->D->B, I would use the variables above
 }
}

 

If you need any additional information, please let me know. I am really stuck/confused by it.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: How to control the labels of associated fields?
entry.content:
In fiori elments I want to include SelectionFields to filter my entity. The entity has an association to another entity which in itself has a chain of associations.

I now want to control the Labels of these fields like this:

annotate service.MyEntity with @(
    UI.SelectionFields : [
        field1_material_customer_customerfield,
        field1_material_ID,
        field1_message_ID,
    ],
);

annotate service.MyEntity with {
    // header .Label : 'header_message_ID',
    field1_material_customer_customerfield .Label: '{i18n>customer}'
};

The entity I annotate has the field "field1" as an association to the other entity. This entity has the field e.g. "material" as an association.

Hope this makes it clear.

Best regards

entry.title: Max Success Plan at SAP Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Join SAP executives, industry experts, and peers at SAP Sapphire 2026 for the reveal of SAP’s bold new AI vision and a reimagined Joule experience. The flagship event features onsite events in Orlando (May 11–13) and Madrid (May 19–21), plus a virtual edition (May 12–13) to optimize your business transformation journey through exceptional keynotes, peer networking and live demos.

As SAP is entering a new era of services and support, we’re excited to bring the Max Success Plan to Orlando and Madrid. With dedicated sessions showcasing how our success plan can be a catalyst for enterprise-wide transformation and our exclusive Max Lounges offering an intimate setting with our top executives to explore our services and learn about the recently launched Max Success Plan.

Max Lounges

The Max Lounge is a dedicated space for face‑to‑face conversations, focused on engagement alignment, feedback exchange, and an overview of Max Success Plan services. Connect with your SAP account team to schedule a dedicated meeting in our Max Lounge, where you can discuss your transformation priorities.

Max Success Plan sessions

Don’t miss out on our Max Success Plan and SAP MaxAttention sessions focused on driving value creation and enabling your strategic business transformation. We will update the list of sessions dedicated to Max Success Plan here and you can also find the full session catalog for each event below:

SAP Sapphire Orlando:

  • Tuesday, May 12 (2:00 – 2:20 p.m.):  Customer Success Story: Scaling success: Coke One’s confident SAP S/4HANA software transformation | SER1251 (also featured at the virtual edition) 
  • Tuesday, May 12 (10:00 – 11:15 a.m.): Customer Success Story: SAP’s generative AI boosts efficiency: Sales order automation at Corteva | FOU1780 
  • Session catalog 

SAP Sapphire Madrid:

  • Session catalog

Join the exclusive ASUG pre-conference on the new SAP Services and Support portfolio

The immersive event brings together SAP leaders, product experts, and customers to walk through the modernized SAP Services and Support portfolio, explain the new tiered success plans, and demonstrate how organizations can accelerate cloud adoption, improve operations, and drive measurable business outcomes. Find out more in this dedicated blog post on Unlocking Transformational Value at SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando.

entry.title: Maintaining serial numbers during Returns order creation
entry.content:
Hi Team,

I am currently working on two customer returns scenarios in SAP S/4HANA public Cloud:

  1. After the goods are returned and inspected, if they are found to be not in acceptable condition, they should be moved to scrap.

  2. After the goods are returned and inspected, if they are in acceptable condition, they should be moved to unrestricted (free) stock.

For both scenarios, I am creating a returns order (document type: CBAR) and selecting the follow-up activity “Receive into Plant.”
With this setting, the stock is posted to blocked stock after goods receipt, and the inspection process is carried out subsequently.

However, I am facing an issue with serial number entry:

  • When using “Receive into Plant,” I do not see an option to enter serial numbers in the returns order.

  • In contrast, when using follow-up activities such as “Immediately Move to Scrap” or “Immediately Move to Specified Stock,” the system triggers an incompleteness log for serial numbers, allowing manual entry at the order level.

I would like to understand:

  • Why does this difference in behavior occur based on the selected follow-up activity?

  • How can serial numbers be captured in the returns order when using “Receive into Plant”?

  • Is there any configuration or standard approach to ensure serial number tracking at the returns order level in this scenario?

Additionally, I noticed that standard APIs for returns order creation seem to support serial number input.
Could you please confirm whether using these APIs would allow creation of returns orders with serial numbers for this scenario?

Thank you for your support.

entry.title: Re: Problems with USING ALL CLIENTS
entry.content: Hi Kevin, I think nowadays i would write that with cds-views and table-functions. In a table function you can write the sql in sqlscript (similar to abap, but has far more capapilities than regular abap in sql-expressions). In sqlscript you can use different joins and use the client-column in every-join. In sql-script you must not write "client specified" or "using all clients". Instead in the underlying cds-view you declare the client as follows, if you want to give the client-numbers dynamically: <pre> @ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_DEPENDENT define table function zb_my_select_tf with parameters @Environment.systemField: #CLIENT i_client : vdm_v_sap_client </pre>

entry.title: Jumpstart Your HR Transformation with SAP Signavio Value Accelerators
entry.content:
The  SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library (VAL) provides ready-to-use, SAP-aligned process content that helps organizations move faster and with less risk during transformation programs. For SAP SuccessFactors customers and partners, standard, well-structured process content is very valuable: it reduces ambiguity during fit-to-standard workshops, creates a single source of truth for process owners and implementers, and shortens the time from discovery to design and deployment. 

The value accelerator for SAP SuccessFactors can directly be downloaded from VAL. In addition to that, the SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL), offers the opportunity for everybody to explore the value accelerator without the prerequisite of an own workspace. Built in SAP Signavio, the SSPL is the foundation for process design and standardization in the SAP SuccessFactors domain.  

No matter whether viewed via VAL or SSPL, the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator for Process Design and Standardization for SAP SuccessFactors  delivers more than just flowcharts: it provides interactive process artifacts that expose roles, system capabilities, data elements, AI-driven activities, and recommended actions for key moments that matter in your HR transformation journey. 

AI at the Heart of Modern HR Processes 

A standout feature of this release is the comprehensive "Driven by AI" attribute, which highlights how artificial intelligence supports your HR processes across three distinct categories: 

  • Deep Learning AI: Transform talent development and employee experience with personalized recommendations, predictions, and deep insights 
  • Generative AI: Accelerate HR processes and tasks by quickly generating or summarizing text across the SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite 
  • Joule (with Navigational and Transactional use cases): Deliver a better employee experience while lowering personnel costs with a natural language processing AI copilot 

These AI capabilities are clearly marked throughout the process models, helping you identify opportunities to leverage intelligent automation in your HR operations. 

Further Key Benefits at a Glance 

  • Fast ramp-up for SAP SuccessFactors transformation programs: Jump into fit-to-standard workshops with structured, SAP-aligned models instead of starting from scratch 
  • Improved alignment between HR operations, people managers, and IT through a common process language and clearly defined handovers 
  • Process harmonization: The layered architecture and navigation maps make it easy to identify overlaps and inconsistencies across regions and business units 
  • Solution design and configuration: BPMN models include system handoffs and data artifacts that help architects identify integration needs and data mapping requirements 
  • And more: Training and adoption support, reusable, governance-ready assets, ... 

Who Should Use This Content? 

This value accelerator is designed for: 

  • HR transformation teams and business process owners 
  • SAP SuccessFactors implementation partners and consultants 
  • IT architects and solution designers 
  • Business transformation managers and process excellence teams 
  • Anyone involved in HR digitalization and standardization initiatives 

What’s Included 

As described by @Marijn in the blog “Gain a comprehensive understanding of the processes in the SAP SuccessFactors process library”, the content comes with many useful artifacts: 

End-to-End Process Views and Hierarchies 

Within the SAP Signavio Process Collaboration Hub, you can explore processes in two main ways: 

  • End-to-End Process View: Navigate the complete HR process landscape 
  • Process Hierarchy: Found under "Shared Documents" in the left-hand panel  

Three New End-to-End Journey Models 

Additionally, there are three new journey models now available in the "Process Journeys" folder: 

  1. Recruit to Onboard 
  2. Develop to Grow 
  3. Reward to Retain 

These journeys help you introduce the SAP SuccessFactors process framework in a clear, structured, and business-friendly way. Each journey provides a high-level overview of the process landscape and serves as a visual guide to the roles, applications, and subprocesses involved, perfect for establishing shared understanding across stakeholders and accelerating onboarding for teams unfamiliar with SAP Signavio or SAP SuccessFactors. 

Note: Journey Model content is only available in SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL), not in the content downloadable from SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library. 

Rich Process Attributes  

Each process and activity includes detailed metadata: 

At the Process Level: 

  • Short Description, Purpose, Prerequisites, and Success Criteria 
  • Linkage to Business and Solution Capabilities 
  • AI support indicators 

Simply select a process of interest by clicking the “+” symbol and open up the panel on the right side to get the information. 

 

At the Activity Level: 

  • Fuchsia desktop icon: Indicates the SAP SuccessFactors capability used 
  • Orange person icon: Marks a "Moment that Matter" 
  • Cyan cogwheel icon: Denotes support by SAP Business AI 

Overlays highlight these attributes, while details can be explored by clicking on an activity which opens up the panel on the right side. 

Process Roles: 
Each swim lane represents a responsible role. Selecting a swim lane reveals: 

  • Role Description 
  • Process Role Group (e.g., HR or Business) 
  • Process Role Category: 
    • Business: "Manager" or "Individual Contributor" 
    • HR: Based on Dave Ulrich’s 3-pillar model: "HR Consultancy," "HR Expertise," "HR Operations," plus "HR IT" 

Data Elements: 
Activities may be linked to input/output documents. When selecting a document, the side panel displays - in addition to the description: 

  • Data Type (SuccessFactors, 3rd party, or non-system data) 
  • Data Element (for SAP SuccessFactors data) 
  • Data Element Grouping (e.g., EC data grouped under Personal or Employment Information) 

 

Downloadable Process Summaries 

Based on customer feedback, we’ve added one-pager process summaries containing: 

  • Key metadata for each process 
  • Direct link to the respective process in the SAP Signavio Collaboration Hub 

How to Access the Content 

Getting started is simple: 

  • Explore in SAP SuccessFactors process library (SSPL): Visit the SAP SuccessFactors Process Library in Signavio to explore the content interactively without needing your own workspace 
  • Download from the Value Accelerator Library (VAL): Download the complete package directly from VAL for use in your own SAP Signavio workspace.  

  • Watch the demo: Check out  this video for a complete walkthrough on how to explore the content in SSPL and download it from VAL

 

For a first overview, we encourage you to explore the journeys in the SAP SuccessFactors process library and use them as a starting point when introducing processes to your stakeholders. Additionally, you can leverage the process summaries for quick reference and stakeholder communication. 

You can look into these resources should you require further information on how to work with the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator Library:  

  • L1 Deck with overview of SAP Signavio Value Accelerators and their benefits. 
  • User guide for VAL on SAP Help. 
  • For more information on the SAP SuccessFactors process library, please refer to the SAP SuccessFactors Community. 

A big thank you to everyone who contributed to this release! Special kudos to the authors of the SAP SuccessFactors process content for their detailed modeling and practical guidance. Share your feedback with your HCM Business Partner or send us an email to SSPL@sap.com  to help us refine and improve these resources to drive future enhancements 

Ready to Transform Your HR Processes? 

Explore the SAP Signavio Value Accelerator for process design and standardization for SAP SuccessFactors today and turn process complexity into competitive advantage!

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay - history Assigment data
entry.content: I have a couple of thoughts. 1. WHY???, what are they trying to accomplish by determining the BU that includes the most days worked? I would get an understanding of the requirement and determine if this is really necessary. 2. How many BU's do they have? If it is a manageable number, you can use conditional logic to say something like: if(BU=BU1, # of days, else 0) at the assignment level then aggregate at the entry level to determine which column is the greatest. Once they have determined the BU, what are they going to do with the information? You should understand the end game before trying to build anything because it may be unnecessary work.

Tomas

entry.title: Alphanumeric CNPJ: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
Brazil’s tax reform introduces several structural changes across the fiscal landscape, and one of the most impactful is the adoption of the alphanumeric CNPJ. As this new identifier takes shape, SAP systems must be ready.

This blog provides an overview of the change, explains why it is being introduced, outlines the SAP areas affected, and highlights the delivery timelines and key references you should be aware of to prepare for compliance.

What Is Changing?

The Brazilian CNPJ (National Register of Legal Entities) has always been a 14-digit numeric identifier. Starting in July 2026, it will expand to include both letters and numbers, becoming an alphanumeric identifier.

This change aims to:

  • Increase the available combination space for taxpayer identification
  • Improve the classification of entities, branches, and establishments
  • Provide more flexibility and scalability for the new tax model

SAP's Preparation and Timeline

SAP is actively updating its solutions to accommodate the new format. The central reference is SAP Note 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note, which consolidates guidance, impacted objects, delivery plans, and related documentation.

This is the primary source you should follow.

Delivery plans can be accessed through the following links:

  • Tax Reform - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Normative Instruction No. 2229, of October 15,
  • Tax Reform - Joint Technical Note 2025.001 - Alphanumeric CNPJ

Key Areas Affected

SAP has identified several modules and processes affected by the new CNPJ format. Key areas include:

  • Business Partner: Fields now support alphanumeric input with updated validation rules and error messages
    • Adjustment delivered in SAP Note 3583627
  • Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database: Structural updates to tables, NF-e repository, integration frameworks and tax transactions
  • Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e): Updates to electronic nota fiscal, digital signature, and schema validations
  • Human Capital Management (HCM) Brazil (Payroll): Adaptation of eSocial solution (layouts, event tables, and extraction process), employer records, employee master data and HCM reports
  • Finance and Banking: Changes to CNAB files, PIX, TED, and Boleto payment methods, updates in routines for generating payment files

SAP System Changes in Customizing (IMG)

Adjustments already delivered within the SAP Customizing environment include:

Global Company Parameters

SPRO → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Global Parameters → Update Additional Parameters

Business Place Definition

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → General Application Functions → Business Place → Define Business Places

These areas now fully support alphanumeric CNPJ values.

ABAP Objects Impacted

SAP Note 3714871 provides a detailed list of ABAP objects expected to be modified. These objects are being converted from numeric to character-based (CHAR) fields to support the new identifier.

SAP recommends that you review custom developments (Z objects) and integrations that reference numeric CNPJ fields, as these may need adjustments.

This includes:

  • Tables
  • Data elements
  • Domain definitions
  • Interfaces
  • Business Add-In (BAdI) implementations
  • Enhancements
  • Custom reports

Planned Delivery Timeline

Important: The expected delivery dates are subject to change without prior notice and represent only an estimate provided by SAP, not a contractual commitment for delivery.

HCM Brazil (Payroll)

  • Phase 1: March 13, 2026
  • Phase 2: March 31, 2026

Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments

  • Planned delivery: April 17, 2026 (Prerequisite for all other Logistics, Document and Reporting Compliance, and Financial updates)

Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e) – Public Cloud and Private Cloud/On-Premise

  • Planned delivery: April 17, 2026

Business Partner and Statutory Reports

  • Planned delivery: April 17, 2026

Banking Files – CNAB / Payment Methods

  • CNAB Private Cloud/On-Premise: April 16, 2026FICA Private Cloud/On-Premise (PIX, TED, Boleto included): April 16, 2026

Key References

SAP Note

  • 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note

Regulatory Change Manager Links

  • HCM Impacts – Phase 1 and Phase 2
  • Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments
  • Business Partner and Statutory Reports
  • CNAB Files
  • CNAB Files FICA

Note: The links provided is specific to the Product Impact of S/4HANA Private Cloud. For other products, such as S/4HANA Private Cloud/on‑premise, SAP ERP, SAP S/4 Public Cloud, refer to the regulatory change manager links related to each product.

What Should You Do Now?

Start by reviewing SAP Note 3714871 and evaluating any custom developments that reference CNPJ fields. Early preparation will help minimize disruption and ensure your organization stays compliant as Brazil's tax infrastructure evolves.

In summary, your action plan should be:

  1. Review SAP Note 3714871 in detail
  2. Evaluate all custom developments that reference CNPJ fields
  3. Track delivery updates via the regulatory change manager
  4. Plan for early testing in sandbox environment
  5. Update internal processes and integrations (banks, partners, NF-e service providers, tax engines, etc.)

Early preparation will help you avoid disruption and maintain compliance as Brazil's tax landscape evolves. The clock is ticking, but with the right approach, you can navigate this transition smoothly.

For more information or if you have any questions about the Brazilian tax reform,  visit the dedicated Tax Reform Work Zone — your hub for updates, resources, and deeper insights.

Did you enjoy this post? Give it a Like and share it with your colleagues! Feel free to leave your feedback or comments in the space below. And remember to follow the SAP S/4HANA Logistics for Brazil tag in the SAP Community to stay tuned for the latest news. 

 
Thanks for reading — until next time!
Luize
